script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Ezequiel, you need to be more careful when you're reporting on sensitive stories. I've seen some of the fluff pieces you've done for the news station.

script.speaker: Ezequiel
script.text: What are you talking about? My pieces are fine. They get good ratings.

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Ratings aren't everything. Your aunt works in the mayor's office and she told me that they've been getting complaints about the way you reported on that last city project. You need to consider the consequences of what you say and how it reflects on our family.

script.speaker: Ezequiel
script.text: That's not fair. You're always on my case about something. Can't you just support me for once? I'm trying to make a career out of this.

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: I am supporting you, but I'm also trying to teach you about the responsibility that comes with the job. You can't just report whatever you want without thinking about who it's going to affect. It's not just about you; it's about our family's reputation. Remember when your mom was a journalist and got in trouble for reporting some confidential information?

script.speaker: Ezequiel
script.text: Yeah... but that was a long time ago. And mom's not even in the business anymore. You're always bringing up the past and -

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: - and what I'm saying is that the principles are still the same. You need to be careful and considerate in your reporting. You don't want to end up like your mom, having to leave the profession because of one mistake.

script.speaker: Ezequiel
script.text: I don't want to talk about this anymore...

script.speaker: Malik
script.text: Dude, I'm telling you, I've been taking it easy on the drinks lately, okay?

script.speaker: Heriberto
script.text: - Yeah, right, that's why you got wasted at the company party last week.

script.speaker: Malik
script.text: That was one night, man. I've been stressed out with all this new work coming in, and just needed to let loose. Plus, I've been really worried about that presentation we have coming up... performance anxiety's been killing me.

script.speaker: Heriberto
script.text: Performance anxiety? Come on, Malik, you're one of the most charismatic guys I know. Besides, I'm more worried about you not taking care of yourself. As friends, I want to make sure you're not burning out.

script.speaker: Malik
script.text: I... appreciate that, Heri, but you're not exactly one to lecture about self-care, considering how much you work too. I mean, I've seen you during those late-night coding sessions... when's the last time you took a break?

script.speaker: Heriberto
script.text: Hey, that's different. I'm just trying to get ahead. But seriously, Malik, that's not the point. The point is, your drinking habits are starting to concern me. Can we at least agree to keep an eye on each other, as friends?

script.speaker: Malik
script.text: Yeah... yeah, I guess you're right. I just need to find some better ways to manage my stress, I guess.

script.speaker: David
script.text: Tamika, have you seen my mail? I've been waiting for that tax refund

script.speaker: Tamika
script.text: Uh, I think it's on the kitchen counter... wait, were you gonna put that towards the rent? Because I was thinking we could split it differently this month

script.speaker: David
script.text: No, actually I was planning to use it to pay off some of my student loans. I'm trying to get my finances in order before I get married next year

script.speaker: Tamika
script.text: Married? Whoa, I had no idea. You're getting married? I mean, I'm happy for you, but- didn't you just start dating her?

script.speaker: David
script.text: -we've been together for three years, actually. And yeah, I know it's fast, but we're just really sure about each other. Plus, her family is kinda pushing for it, I guess... anyway, what's your point with the rent?

script.speaker: Tamika
script.text: Just that I'm not sure if I can afford it on my own if you're moving out after the wedding. We've been splitting the bills 50/50, and I don't know if I can handle the full rent by myself

script.speaker: David
script.text: Huh, I didn't think about that. I guess we should start looking for a new roommate or something... I don't know, we can figure it out later

script.speaker: Tamika
script.text: Later? You're talking about your future, David - marriage, kids, the whole thing... you're basically leaving me to deal with this stuff on my own

script.speaker: Christopher
script.text: Jordan, come check this out - I just found the most ridiculous article about how guys are supposed to dress to impress girls.

script.speaker: Jordan
script.text: Wait, what? - let me see that... just give me a sec, I'm in the middle of unpacking these boxes

script.speaker: Christopher
script.text: It's saying stuff like we need to wear tight clothes to show off our muscles - like, what's wrong with just wearing what's comfortable?

script.speaker: Jordan
script.text: That's so stupid... I mean, what's with the stereotypes? Not every guy wants to impress girls - or needs to. And not every girl cares about that stuff either

script.speaker: Christopher
script.text: Exactly! And it's not like we're those kind of guys anyway - we're more laid back than that. I mean, you're always telling me to clean up my act, but it's not like I'm trying to impress anyone

script.speaker: Jordan
script.text: Ha! Yeah, I guess I do say that a lot... but it's just because I care about our space, you know? I don't want our place to look like a mess when people come over. But, I mean, as friends, shouldn't we just be able to be ourselves around each other?

script.speaker: Christopher
script.text: Absolutely... it's just frustrating when society puts all these expectations on us - like, we have to dress a certain way or act a certain way just because we're guys

script.speaker: Jordan
script.text: I know what you mean... Ugh, can you just hand me that box cutter real quick? And pass me that roll of packing tape too, please?

script.speaker: Hang
script.text: Okay Precious, time for bed, your mom told me you've been reading that book for hours, what's so interesting?

script.speaker: Precious
script.text: It's about this princess who disguises herself and- -

script.speaker: Hang
script.text: - yeah, yeah, and saves the kingdom, I'm sure, look, you can finish it tomorrow, sweetie

script.speaker: Precious
script.text: But dad, she's so brave and clever, I love how she outsmarts everyone... I wish I could be like her

script.speaker: Hang
script.text: I'm glad you admire her, but you know you're not allowed to read past 9 PM on school nights, and it's already 9:30

script.speaker: Precious
script.text: Just five more minutes, please, dad? I promise I'll go straight to sleep after... You always say I should appreciate strong characters, and that's exactly what she is

script.speaker: Hang
script.text: Precious, I mean it, no more reading, we'll talk about this in the morning... your teacher's been saying you've been falling asleep in class, and it's not just about the reading, it's about listening to authority

script.speaker: Precious
script.text: But...

script.speaker: Darius
script.text: Mom always said that story about the time I learned to ride a bike was way more impressive than when you did.

script.speaker: Wei
script.text: Wait what? No way -

script.speaker: Darius
script.text: Yeah, and it's true, your first time on a bike was pretty sad, I mean...

script.speaker: Wei
script.text: That's not the point, you always think you're the favorite or whatever, but what really annoys me is how you always try to take over when we talk to Dad about our stories.

script.speaker: Darius
script.text: What, no, I just like to tell it my way because it's funnier, I guess, you always get too anxious and the story gets boring.

script.speaker: Wei
script.text: It gets boring? You're the one who always interrupts me and changes my stories around, do you even remember what really happened that day?

script.speaker: Darius
script.text: Uh, pretty much... I mean...

script.speaker: Wei
script.text: Exactly, you don't, and it's not just about the stories, it's about how you always try to be the center of attention. And when we were kids, you would always get us in trouble by talking back to Mom and Dad.

script.speaker: Darius
script.text: That's not fair, I was just standing up for what's right, you were always the obedient one, and I think that's why they liked your stories less.

script.speaker: Wei
script.text: Liked them less? You're kidding right? They liked them more because I actually put effort into telling them how they were, not just making myself sound cool.

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Grandpa, why do you think imagination is so overrated in writing? You said that in your writing group the other day...

script.speaker: Richard
script.text: Not overrated, kiddo. Just that sometimes I think people rely too heavily on it and forget about the craft. There's a difference between-

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: -between imagination and creativity, yeah? But don't you think that's what makes literature so amazing? I mean, look at Gabriel Garcia Marquez, he practically invented magical realism...

script.speaker: Richard
script.text: -Magical realism, right... well, that's a whole different story. Garcia Marquez was a master, no doubt. But when you're writing, you need a balance between letting your imagination run wild and restraining it with good storytelling techniques... You can't just have a bunch of fanciful ideas floating around without any structure.

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: But that's the thing – I think structure can stifle imagination. When I'm writing, I want to see where my ideas take me, not be bound by some formula. It's like what Ray Bradbury said: 'You must stay drunk on writing so reality cannot destroy you...'

script.speaker: Richard
script.text: Bradbury, huh? Well, he was a talented writer, no argument there. However, as your grandpa, I'm also worried about you getting too caught up in the romantic notion of writing. You know, when I was in college, we didn't have all these... writing groups and workshops. We just wrote because we had to, and we figured it out as we went.

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: I know, grandpa... but times have changed. And writing's not the same for me as it was for you. I'm just trying to find my own way, you know?

script.speaker: Mariela
script.text: I don't get why you're not taking charge on this project, Jermaine. You've been talking about it for weeks.

script.speaker: Jermaine
script.text: I am taking charge, it's just... I need the team to be on board with my ideas. We can't just have one person calling all the shots.

script.speaker: Mariela
script.text: But you're the one who's been doing all the research. I think people will trust your lead. Besides, I've already started working on the marketing strategy, so it seems like things are moving forward.

script.speaker: Jermaine
script.text: - Yeah, but that's exactly what I'm talking about. I didn't ask you to start working on marketing yet... I was thinking we could-...

script.speaker: Mariela
script.text: Oh, come on Jermaine, I was just trying to help. You know how I feel about teamwork. Don't be so possessive about your ideas.

script.speaker: Jermaine
script.text: I'm not being possessive, I just... I don't know, maybe it's because we're still getting to know each other. It feels like we're not totally on the same page about our roles in this project.

script.speaker: Mariela
script.text: Getting to know each other? We've been dating for months. I'd say we're doing okay. And our roles should be pretty clear - we're partners on this project, just like we're partners outside of work.

script.speaker: Jermaine
script.text: Partners, yeah... but sometimes I feel like we're not communicating like we should be, you know? Maybe we should set some ground rules for how we're gonna work on this project.

script.speaker: Liam
script.text: You know, I have to say, it's really interesting how everyone's perception of beauty is different. I mean, I was talking to Sarah the other day and she was saying how she thinks the girl who just moved in downstairs is gorgeous, but to me, I just don't see it

script.speaker: Kimberly
script.text: Wait, you mean Emily? I think she's really pretty too. I've seen her around the building a few times

script.speaker: Liam
script.text: Exactly, pretty, but not gorgeous. I mean, I don't know, maybe I'm just not attracted to that type or something. But it's weird because Sarah was saying how she thinks we'd be perfect for each other

script.speaker: Kimberly
script.text: Ha, SARAH. Always trying to play matchmaker. I swear, she thinks our apartment is her own personal dating show or something

script.speaker: Liam
script.text: Right? But for real though, do you think I'm that kind of guy? I mean, I value our friendship and all, but I don't know if I'm ready for anything serious right now

script.speaker: Kimberly
script.text: slow down - we're not even talking about you and Emily dating. But, as your roommate, I think you should at least give her a chance, see if there's anything there. And who knows, maybe you'll learn something new about yourself

script.speaker: Liam
script.text: Yeah maybe... but I dunno, it's just, as friends, can't we just hang out and not have to deal with all the drama that comes with dating?

script.speaker: Kimberly
script.text: that's the thing, Liam - I think you're overthinking it. just... go talk to her or something. Geez

script.speaker: Jordan
script.text: I'm telling you, Jermaine, it's just rude to be on your phone the whole time we're hanging out.

script.speaker: Jermaine
script.text: What's the big deal? I'm just checking my messages real quick...

script.speaker: Jordan
script.text: Real quick? You've been scrolling through Instagram for like 10 minutes. It feels like you're not even present.

script.speaker: Jermaine
script.text: -present? Come on, babe, I am present... I just need to -

script.speaker: Jordan
script.text: No, you're not. And it's not just about us, it's about basic politeness. We're at a restaurant, and you're ignoring the waiter... our food's been sitting there for like 5 minutes.

script.speaker: Jermaine
script.text: Oh, I'll get to it... I just... You know, work's been stressing me out, and I just need to clear my head. Can't a guy just have one minute to himself?

script.speaker: Jordan
script.text: One minute? It's been way longer than that. And, honestly, it feels like you're choosing your phone over me... over us. Like, remember when we first started dating, and we would actually talk and enjoy each other's company?

script.speaker: Jermaine
script.text: Jordan, that's not fair... I'm sorry, okay? I'll put my phone away.

script.speaker: Thu
script.text: I had a weird encounter at the coffee shop today. This woman just assumed I was the nanny for the kids with me. Like, I get it, I'm Vietnamese and the kids are white, but still - it's so infuriating.

script.speaker: David
script.text: Yeah, I can imagine. But what did you expect her to think?

script.speaker: Thu
script.text: Expect her to think? I don't know... maybe don't make assumptions based on my ethnicity? I was just trying to get us a table and she's asking me about the kids' parents... -

script.speaker: David
script.text: I'm just saying, it's not always about racism or sexism. Sometimes people are just trying to make conversation.

script.speaker: Thu
script.text: Not always about racism or sexism? You're kidding, right? That's exactly what it's about. And you know what really gets me? You didn't even notice. You're always like, 'Oh, people are just people,' but that's because you're a man. You don't experience it.

script.speaker: David
script.text: Whoa, that's not fair. I'm just trying to understand the situation -

script.speaker: Thu
script.text: - understand the situation? You've never even tried to understand what it's like to be a woman in a patriarchal society. To be constantly objectified and marginalized. You just assume that because we're partners, you get it, but you don't.

script.speaker: David
script.text: Okay, fine. I don't get it. But that doesn't mean I'm not trying. Can we please just talk about this without getting defensive?

script.speaker: Thu
script.text: I'm not being defensive, I'm being honest. And you need to listen.

script.speaker: Lakeisha
script.text: Joel's birthday is coming up, and his mom's stressing out about the guest list

script.speaker: Jalen
script.text: What's the big deal? Just invite whoever

script.speaker: Lakeisha
script.text: Yeah, I told her that, but... I don't know... family dynamics can be weird sometimes

script.speaker: Jalen
script.text: As friends, we've been through this with our own families. Maybe I can give her some tips

script.speaker: Lakeisha
script.text: I don't know... I already told her you'd probably crash the party anyway, so she's expecting you to come...

script.speaker: Jalen
script.text: True, but I mean, the situation's complicated... what if her ex shows up?

script.speaker: Lakeisha
script.text: Jalen, that's a whole different conversation. I'm trying to say she's worried about you and Joel's sister, Maya

script.speaker: Jalen
script.text: -Joel's sister? Oh, you mean because of what happened at the game last year?

script.speaker: Lakeisha
script.text: Not that exactly, but just... she doesn't know how to handle the social etiquette with you two around each other now

script.speaker: Jalen
script.text: Lakeisha, we're adults. We can handle ourselves

script.speaker: Lakeisha
script.text: I know, but you know how it is when family relationships get involved... there's always drama

script.speaker: Jalen
script.text: Fine, put me on the guest list, and if Maya wants to talk about it, I'll talk

script.speaker: Tamika
script.text: Honestly, Lucia, I'm starting to get worried about your drinking habits. You're always saying you need a drink after work, but then a drink turns into three, four... you know?

script.speaker: Lucia
script.text: My job's been super stressful, Tamika. I've been feeling really overwhelmed. And don't even get me started on the performance reviews that are coming up. You know how much I hate being put on the spot like that... -

script.speaker: Tamika
script.text: - I do, but that's exactly my point. You can't just rely on booze to calm your nerves. We've talked about this before, and I feel like we're not making progress. You're going to end up with a problem if you don't find healthier ways to cope.

script.speaker: Lucia
script.text: I know, I know... it's just that everyone at the office does it. We go out and have a few drinks after work. It's not that big of a deal. And anyway, you're not exactly one to lecture about stress management. Remember that time you freaked out about the mortgage payments?

script.speaker: Tamika
script.text: That's totally different, Lucia. I wasn't self-medicating with booze every night. And we talked about our budget and figured it out together. You're not even willing to have that conversation with me about your drinking... it feels like you're shutting me out.

script.speaker: Jermaine
script.text: Dude, I'm trying to make breakfast in here. Can you please give me some space?

script.speaker: Huy
script.text: Sorry, I was just looking for my folder real quick. I've got a meeting at 9 and I still need to review some stuff -

script.speaker: Jermaine
script.text: Review it on the couch or something, it smells like burnt toast in here. How many times do I need to remind you to clean up after yourself?

script.speaker: Huy
script.text: I did clean up, Jermaine... Come on, I'm just having a bad morning, don't make it worse...

script.speaker: Jermaine
script.text: Your bad morning is about to get worse if you don't start pulling your weight around here. We've talked about this before, you can't just leave everything for me to do on the weekends. It's not fair.

script.speaker: Huy
script.text: Okay, fine... But can we discuss this later? I really need to focus on this meeting... And by the way, I did the laundry last night. You're welcome.

script.speaker: Jermaine
script.text: Thanks for the laundry, but that doesn't make up for the fact that I'm always cooking and cleaning while you're out doing your own thing. We need to find a better balance, man. This isn't a hotel.

script.speaker: Huy
script.text: Alright, alright... I get it... Sorry, okay?

script.speaker: Jermaine
script.text: That's not an apology, that's just saying sorry so I'll shut up. Look, let's just take a break and talk about this when you get back from your meeting, okay?

script.speaker: Trung
script.text: Dude, I'm telling you, I've got way too much on my plate right now. Mom's always on my case about helping out with the family business, and I've got a ton of schoolwork to catch up on.

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: That's not my problem, Trung. We're working on this group project together, and you're just dumping all the work on me. I'm not your personal assistant.

script.speaker: Trung
script.text: I'm not asking you to do it all, just help me out a bit. I've been doing this for years, but now that you're home from college, you can lend a hand...

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: - Yeah, yeah, I know, but that's exactly my point. I'm home for a reason, to help out, but not to take over everything. You can't just expect me to pick up where you left off without even discussing it with me first.

script.speaker: Trung
script.text: I'm discussing it with you now, aren't I? You're always talking about how we need to work together as a team, and then when I ask for your help, you get all defensive...

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Defensive? You think I'm being defensive? You're the one who's always trying to control everything. Remember when we were kids, and you would always try to boss me around? It's the same thing now.

script.speaker: Trung
script.text: That was a long time ago, Alex. I'm not trying to boss you around, I just need some help. Can we please just try to work together on this?

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Fine, but we need to set some boundaries and actually collaborate, not just do things your way. And maybe next time, just ask me instead of assuming I'll do all the work.

script.speaker: Liam
script.text: Vy, I've been noticing you've been coming home pretty late on weekdays, you know, and I'm starting to get a bit worried. Is everything alright?

script.speaker: Vy
script.text: Everything's fine, Liam. I just... I don't know, sometimes I need to unwind after work, you know?

script.speaker: Liam
script.text: Unwind? With a drink or two, or three? I don't want you to turn out like your uncle, Vy -

script.speaker: Vy
script.text: - Uncle? What's that supposed to mean? You always bring him up whenever we talk about this...

script.speaker: Liam
script.text: It means I care about you and I see the same patterns emerging. I've seen what stress and performance anxiety do to people, and I don't want that for you. Your job is demanding enough, don't make it worse with drinking.

script.speaker: Vy
script.text: - But it helps me relax, Liam. You don't know what it's like to have to meet those sales targets, to have my boss breathing down my neck... Sometimes I feel like I'm losing myself, you know?

script.speaker: Liam
script.text: Losing yourself? Vy, your mom and I worked hard to provide for you, to give you a good life... I just wish you could talk to me about this stuff instead of trying to drown it in booze.

script.speaker: Vy
script.text: Drown it? Liam, come on... You're not going to lecture me about responsibility, are you?

script.speaker: Trinh
script.text: Hey Emma, have you seen the new task list from management? I swear, they're just piling it on us without any regard for our work-life balance

script.speaker: Emma
script.text: Uh, no not yet. What's on it?

script.speaker: Trinh
script.text: Just more of the same - take on extra responsibilities, produce more results, but God forbid they give us a raise. It's like they think we're just going to magically be more efficient without any actual support

script.speaker: Emma
script.text: That's just how corporate works, Trinh. Don't be so cynical

script.speaker: Trinh
script.text: Cynical? I'm being realistic. And it's not just about the company - have you seen the way they treat the temps and freelancers? No job security, no benefits... it's like they're just disposable

script.speaker: Emma
script.text: I get what you're saying, but we can't just quit our jobs because of... -

script.speaker: Trinh
script.text: -because of what? Because it's not just about us? Because there are people out there who are really struggling? We can't just ignore the bigger picture, Emma

script.speaker: Emma
script.text: -No, I was saying we can't just quit our jobs because we have bills to pay, okay? And as your girlfriend, I'm a little concerned about how this is going to affect our future plans. We need stability

script.speaker: Trinh
script.text: Our future plans? You mean like the plans we've been talking about for our own business? Because if that's what you're worried about, then I think we need to have a bigger conversation about what we want and what we're willing to sacrifice for it...

script.speaker: Jordan
script.text: I know you don't like it, but the scene where she's supposed to be crying just feels so fake to me... I mean, I've watched the rehearsal tapes and it looks like she's trying too hard.

script.speaker: Alejandra
script.text: Jordan, we've been over this, you can't just rewrite the script on a whim. We have a production to put on, and it's already been finalized.

script.speaker: Jordan
script.text: But as the writer, I feel like I have a responsibility to make sure it feels authentic... And you know how much I value your opinion, Mom, but in this case, can't you just trust me?

script.speaker: Alejandra
script.text: Trust you? Jordan, you're 19 years old and directing your first real production. I'm sure you're talented, but you still have a lot to learn... And as your parent, it's my job to guide you.

script.speaker: Jordan
script.text: Guide me? You're stifling my creativity... - I know you're just trying to help, but sometimes you have to let go and trust that I know what I'm doing.

script.speaker: Alejandra
script.text: - Jordan, that's not fair... I'm only trying to help because I care about you and the success of this production. Fine, make the changes, but if it flops, don't come crying to me...

script.speaker: Jordan
script.text: I won't... Thanks, Mom.

script.speaker: Nia
script.text: Carlos, I've been thinking... maybe I should take a break from painting and focus on graphic design instead?

script.speaker: Carlos
script.text: - hold up, what's going on? You've been stressing about your art classes, but now you want to give up on painting altogether?

script.speaker: Nia
script.text: Well, no... it's just, my mom's been on my case about getting a stable job, and I don't know if I can make a living from my art. I need to think about the future, you know?

script.speaker: Carlos
script.text: Nia, you're 22, not 40. What's the rush? You're talented, and I'm sure you'll figure something out. Besides, we signed a lease on this place in part so you could have a studio – seems kinda wasteful to just abandon it now...

script.speaker: Nia
script.text: I know, I know... it's just, sometimes I feel like I'm just pretending to be this quirky artist, and it's all just a facade. Do you think I'm just being eccentric for the sake of it?

script.speaker: Carlos
script.text: Honestly, Nia, I think you're overthinking it. You're an artist, it's what you do. And yeah, maybe you're a bit eccentric, but that's what I like about you, roommate-wise and friend-wise. Just don't let your mom get in your head, okay?

script.speaker: Diego
script.text: I don't know why we're even arguing about this. I thought we agreed, we're not going to this wedding.

script.speaker: Thu
script.text: - because Sarah's my friend and she invited us. It wouldn't be nice if we didn't show up.

script.speaker: Diego
script.text: I don't care about her expectations. We've been putting off our own vacation plans for months because of your family obligations. When's the last time we took a break for us?

script.speaker: Thu
script.text: A break from what? Our life together? I thought that's what marriage was about, supporting each other and our friends.

script.speaker: Diego
script.text: Supporting each other doesn't mean we have to attend every social event. We can send a gift, a nice card... It's not like we're going to miss the ceremony of the year.

script.speaker: Thu
script.text: You're not taking this seriously. Sarah's going to be really hurt if we don't show up. We've known her since college... As friends, we should be there for her.

script.speaker: Diego
script.text: I know, I know, she's an old friend. But I'm tired of always being the one to accommodate everyone else's plans. Can't we just have some 'us' time?

script.speaker: Thu
script.text: Fine... We'll go to the wedding. But can we at least discuss our vacation plans afterwards? I feel like we've been putting that off for too long.

script.speaker: Xuan
script.text: Emma, why are you always dressing up when we go out with friends? You know we're just going to the park for a picnic.

script.speaker: Emma
script.text: What's wrong with dressing up? I like to look nice. And it's not just about looking nice for other people, it's about feeling confident too.

script.speaker: Xuan
script.text: But honestly, you always stand out in a crowd, even when you're wearing casual clothes. Sometimes I feel like people are more interested in talking to you than me... I mean, I've caught a few of our friends staring at you-

script.speaker: Emma
script.text: -Xuan, that's just ridiculous. You're being paranoid. Besides, I think it's flattering that you're a little possessive.

script.speaker: Xuan
script.text: I'm not being possessive, I just... I don't know, it's weird. Sometimes I wonder if they're really our friends or just friends with you. Does that make any sense?

script.speaker: Emma
script.text: Kind of... But trust me, Xuan, they like you too. And even if they didn't, I do. So that's all that matters, right?

script.speaker: Xuan
script.text: I suppose. But you're not helping by wearing a bright yellow sundress to a picnic.

script.speaker: Emma
script.text: This dress? It's not that bright... Fine, I'll change. But you have to promise me one thing: you have to try to have a good time today, no matter what.

script.speaker: Xuan
script.text: I'll try... I'll try.

script.speaker: Jesus
script.text: I don't know how much more of this I can do, Tamika. I thought we were on the same page with this business collaboration, but I'm starting to feel like I'm shouldering all the work

script.speaker: Tamika
script.text: What are you talking about? I've been doing my part. We discussed all of this before we started

script.speaker: Jesus
script.text: That was six months ago... things have changed. And honestly, I feel like you're not as invested as I am. I mean, remember when we first started talking about this idea over drinks? You were so gung-ho, and now -

script.speaker: Tamika
script.text: Now what? I'm just busy, Jesus, that's all. And as friends, I thought we could be more understanding of each other's situations

script.speaker: Jesus
script.text: Understanding? You're not even returning my calls on time, and when we do talk, it's always 'yeah, yeah, I'll get to it.' I need concrete commitments, Tamika. Our friendship aside, this is a business

script.speaker: Tamika
script.text: - okay, okay, I hear you. But can we not do this right now? I have a meeting in 20 minutes and I still need to prep

script.speaker: Jesus
script.text: Tamika, come on... we need to have this conversation. As friends, I value our relationship, but if this business is going to work, we need to be on the same page

script.speaker: Ngoc
script.text: I'm telling you, Dat, beauty is so subjective. What one person finds attractive, another person might not.

script.speaker: Dat
script.text: I get that, but come on, Ngoc, some people are just objectively more attractive than others.

script.speaker: Ngoc
script.text: Objectively? You can't be serious. I mean, have you seen the way you drool over our neighbor, Rachel?

script.speaker: Dat
script.text: - Drool is a bit much, Ngoc. I just think she's really put together, you know? -

script.speaker: Ngoc
script.text: Put together? As in, she meets your checklist for physical beauty?

script.speaker: Dat
script.text: Well, yeah, I mean, she's got the whole package going on. And it's not just about looks, it's about confidence, personality...

script.speaker: Ngoc
script.text: Personality? We've barely talked to her, Dat. How do you even know she has a personality?

script.speaker: Dat
script.text: I've seen her around, Ngoc. She's friendly, she's outgoing... and she's just really easy on the eyes, okay? Don't read too much into it.

script.speaker: Ngoc
script.text: As your roommate, I'm just saying, maybe you should get to know her beyond surface level before deciding she's the epitome of beauty.

script.speaker: Dat
script.text: Fair enough. But you have to admit, Ngoc, she's definitely caught my eye... and I think our friendship can handle me having a crush on the neighbor

script.speaker: Luis
script.text: I don't get why we have to have a party tonight, Mary. It's been a long week.

script.speaker: Mary
script.text: - Come on, it's my birthday, and Sarah's been planning this thing for weeks. We can't just bail on her.

script.speaker: Luis
script.text: So that's our social obligation now? Throwing parties for our friends? I thought we were trying to save money.

script.speaker: Mary
script.text: Not just that, Luis. Sarah's been there for us when we needed her. Remember when she helped us move in and we didn't even know her that well? I feel like we owe her this.

script.speaker: Luis
script.text: As roommates, can't we set some boundaries? I mean, our friends are always dropping by unannounced, eating our food... it's like, no one even asks us if it's okay anymore.

script.speaker: Mary
script.text: That's just what friends do, Luis. We're not just roommates, we're friends with these people. If you're feeling overwhelmed, maybe we can set some boundaries, but not on my birthday, okay?

script.speaker: Luis
script.text: Fine, but next time, can we at least talk about it before inviting the whole crew over?

script.speaker: Mary
script.text: Deal. Now help me set up the living room.

script.speaker: Olivia
script.text: I don't think you should be writing about the Watson family's problems, Tameka. It's not right.

script.speaker: Tameka
script.text: Come on, mom... it's a great story. And it's not like I'm making anything up. The whole town knows about their issues.

script.speaker: Olivia
script.text: That's not the point. The point is, you're a journalist now, and you have to think about the impact of your words. You're not just writing some blog or whatever anymore.

script.speaker: Tameka
script.text: - but mom, you're always saying how I should be truthful and transparent in my writing. And that's exactly what I'm doing. You're just mad because it's about someone we know.

script.speaker: Olivia
script.text: I'm not just mad about that, I'm worried about how it's going to affect your relationships with the people in this town. You're not just a journalist, you're my daughter, and people are going to hold you to a different standard.

script.speaker: Tameka
script.text: So now you're saying I should compromise my journalistic integrity because of who I'm related to? That's not fair.

script.speaker: Olivia
script.text: I'm saying be careful, that's all. And consider the consequences of your actions. You're not just writing for some faceless audience, you're writing about real people's lives.

script.speaker: Tameka
script.text: I know that, mom... I'm not a child anymore.

script.speaker: Olivia
script.text: I know you're not, but as your parent, it's still my job to worry about you and guide you when I think you need it.

script.speaker: Daniel
script.text: Hey Michael, can I ask you something? I-

script.speaker: Michael
script.text: What's up?

script.speaker: Daniel
script.text: It's just... the smell of bacon's been lingering in my apartment for a while now. I know you like cooking in the morning, but could you maybe use the exhaust fan or something?

script.speaker: Michael
script.text: I do use the fan, and I'm not the only one who cooks breakfast around here. What's the big deal?

script.speaker: Daniel
script.text: I don't know... it's just really distracting, especially on weekdays when I'm trying to get some work done. By the way, I thought you were supposed to start that new job downtown?

script.speaker: Michael
script.text: -Yeah, I did start it. It's going well. Why?

script.speaker: Daniel
script.text: Because, as your neighbor, I'm also somewhat of a friend, right? And I remember you saying that this job would give you a better schedule, maybe even more flexible hours... but it seems like you're still making a ruckus in the mornings just like before.

script.speaker: Michael
script.text: Look, Daniel, I appreciate your concern, but I think you're overreacting. I'm still getting used to the job, and yeah, things aren't perfect yet, but I'm working on it, okay?

script.speaker: Taylor
script.text: Come on, Latoya, don't downplay the part where Detective James arrests him just to get him alone - that's some messed-up stuff...I mean, that shows real character

script.speaker: Latoya
script.text: -No, its a terrible plot device, every show does it...the writers -

script.speaker: Taylor
script.text: But that's what makes it so amazing! I mean I was on edge the entire time -

script.speaker: Latoya
script.text: Taylor, you have to admit, it feels rushed...I mean the entire storyline was building up for like three seasons and then they just - resolve it in this one episode?

script.speaker: Taylor
script.text: You're not listening - it's not about that... the problem is that it humanizes the detective, makes him more...relatable...we've been watching this show together for years, don't you want to see the characters grow?

script.speaker: Latoya
script.text: You've been getting really into this show lately, almost as much as you're into that new video game...You're starting to sound like some fanboy online... remember when we just watched our favorite shows, without all this analysis?

script.speaker: Taylor
script.text: Latoya, you're the one who started reading all those recaps online, I'm just sharing my thoughts... and don't be like that, this isn't just some show - we invested in it, together

script.speaker: Latoya
script.text: I guess... Sorry, it's just that lately I've been noticing how different we've become - we don't do as much together anymore, the only thing we seem to share now is the couch

script.speaker: Tameka
script.text: Hey, I wanted to thank you for driving mom to her appointment yesterday. I really appreciate it.

script.speaker: Richard
script.text: No problem, it's not like I had anything better to do... - and besides, someone had to.

script.speaker: Tameka
script.text: Come on, Richard. Don't make it sound like such a chore. We're her kids, it's the least we can do. And I know mom's been feeling really grateful lately.

script.speaker: Richard
script.text: Grateful? Yeah right, I swear, she's getting more and more demanding by the day.

script.speaker: Tameka
script.text: That's not fair, Richard. She's just getting older, we need to be more understanding... I mean, remember how she used to stay up late waiting for us to come home when we were in high school?

script.speaker: Richard
script.text: Tameka, I know, okay... and yeah, I appreciate those times too, but sometimes I feel like I'm the only one who's actually doing anything for her. You're always so busy with your own stuff... do you even remember the last time we all had dinner together?

script.speaker: Tameka
script.text: Richard, that's not true... - I mean, we've had dinner together like, um... a few months ago... and besides, just because I'm busy it doesn't mean I'm not thinking about mom.

script.speaker: Richard
script.text: Whatever, just try to be more involved, okay?

script.speaker: Roosevelt
script.text: Hey Deandre, how's the real estate business going? I feel like I see your face on every billboard around here...

script.speaker: Deandre
script.text: Ha, yeah, I've been doing alright. Actually, that reminds me, have you thought about selling your place?

script.speaker: Roosevelt
script.text: Selling? No, I don't think so. I mean, I've lived here for a while, and I like the neighborhood... and the neighbors, of course.

script.speaker: Deandre
script.text: That's exactly why I think you should sell - you're in a prime location, and you could make a killing. Plus, it'd be a great opportunity for me to give back to the community, help someone like you get a good deal...

script.speaker: Roosevelt
script.text: I don't know, Deandre. I've been here since I was a kid, and my family has a lot of history here. You know, my parents met at that little diner down the street... and I used to walk to school from here. This neighborhood's more than just a commodity to me.

script.speaker: Deandre
script.text: I understand where you're coming from, Roosevelt, but sometimes you have to think about the bigger picture. It's business, not personal. We could network, get some other people from the neighborhood involved... it'd be a real community effort.

script.speaker: Roosevelt
script.text: Community effort? Come on, Deandre. You're just trying to butter me up so I'll sell my place to you. I'm not some naive seller who's going to fall for that... and even if I was, I'm not sure I'd want to do business with someone who's more concerned with making a profit than with the people who actually live here.

script.speaker: Deandre
script.text: Hey, that's not fair, Roosevelt. I am concerned about the people here... I'm just trying to make a living, same as you. And as your neighbor, I thought you'd appreciate the offer.

script.speaker: Roosevelt
script.text: I appreciate the offer, Deandre, but I think we should just stick to being neighbors, alright?

script.speaker: Barbara
script.text: I'm really stressed about this new automation system at work. I mean, I know it's supposed to 'streamline' things, but honestly, I think it's just going to replace half our team.

script.speaker: Taylor
script.text: Maybe, but -

script.speaker: Barbara
script.text: - no, just listen, Taylor, I know you think I'm being paranoid, but I've seen it happen to so many people. They bring in some fancy AI, and next thing you know, everyone's getting laid off. And it's not just about job security, it's about who we are. I mean, I'm a writer, not a... a data analyst or whatever.

script.speaker: Taylor
script.text: Barb, you're not going to lose your identity because of some new technology. Besides, we've talked about this before, you're not exactly 'just a writer'. You're a journalist, and journalists adapt.

script.speaker: Barbara
script.text: Adapt? You think it's that easy? I've seen people struggle to make ends meet after losing their jobs to automation. And it's not just about us, it's about the whole societal commentary on what's valuable work and what's not. I mean, have you seen those memes about robots taking over the world? It's funny, but it's also kind of true.

script.speaker: Taylor
script.text: Barb, come on, we can't just live in a world where we're constantly worried about what's going to happen next. We have to focus on what we can control. And by the way, can we please turn off the TV, the news is depressing.

script.speaker: Barbara
script.text: You're not listening to me, Taylor. This isn't just about me; it's about all of us, as roommates, as friends... we need to think about our future, our bills, our rent -

script.speaker: Taylor
script.text: Barb, I said, let's talk about this later, okay? I have a deadline to meet for my project, and I really need to focus.
